title: Group the Objects (by material)

placement.where: replaces NCERT Class 6 Science Chapter 6 'Materials Around Us', Activity 6.2 'Let us group' — students classify 12 everyday objects by the material they are made of

studentControls.control: Tap an object in the tray, then tap a material bin (Metal / Wood / Plastic / Clay)
studentControls.effect: if the bin matches the object's material it locks in with a pop and a sound; the score and progress advance
the bin shakes and flashes red, a wrong sound plays, a mistake is recorded for that object, and the object is NOT placed

studentControls.control: Tap an already-placed object inside a bin
studentControls.effect: returns it to the tray so it can be re-sorted
clears all placements and mistakes and reshuffles the objects into a fresh order

answerKey.rule: Classify each object by the MATERIAL it is made of. A bin only accepts an object whose material matches it.
answerKey.scoring: First-try score = number of objects placed in the correct bin without any wrong attempt (out of 12). Because a wrong bin never accepts an object, the final board is always fully correct.
answerKey.operatorModeNote: ai = focused demo: bins and read-out stay visible, the object tray + action bar are hidden and student input is inert (the agent drives via placeCard()/highlight()); student = full interactive controls. Default student.
answerKey.agentNote: placeCard accepts a single object id (placeCard('key')) OR an array (placeCard(['key','coin','bolt'])) — each object is routed to its correct material bin, so several can be credited at once. sort() and tap() are aliases.

additionalProps.seed: optional number to fix the shuffle order (defaults to a fresh random order each play)

scientificFacts: Objects can be grouped (classified) by a shared property — here, the material they are made of.
Metals such as iron and brass are hard and shiny; wood is light and does not shine; plastic is light, bendy and never rusts; clay is shaped and then baked hard (a brick is baked clay).
Sorting things into groups by a common property is called classification, and it helps us study and compare materials.
The same object set can be grouped by other properties too, such as whether they float or sink, or are transparent or opaque.
